coredata.dc:title: Tuning surface states to achieve the modulated fluorescence of carbon dots for probing the activity of alkaline phosphatase and immunoassay of α-fetoprotein
coredata.dc:creator: Fang, Xin
Li, Xiao-Qin
Wang, Hong
Wu, Xiu-Ming
Wang, Guang-Li
coredata.dc:description: A convenient redox reaction was utilized to tune the surface states of carbon dots (CDs), leading to a reversibly fluorescent nanoswitch. The oxidizing treatment of CDs by KMnO4 brought about partial oxidation of hydroxyl groups to carboxyl groups, resulting in effective fluorescence quenching; reversely, the subsequent addition of ascorbic acid (AA) restored the quenched fluorescence by the reduction of the previously oxidized carboxyl groups to hydroxyl groups. Based on the redox modulated fluorescence of CDs, quantitative evaluation of alkaline phosphatase (ALP) activity in a wide range from 0.002 to 250U/L with a low detection limit of 6.0×10−4 U/L was realized. In addition, this testing protocol was also expanded to an immunoassay (taking α-fetoprotein (AFP) as an example) by the cascade amplification strategy combing the hybridization chain reaction (HCR) and the ALP–based biocatalysis. A wide linear range of 1.0 fg/mL to 1.0ng/mL with a quite low detection limit of 0.2 fg/mL were obtained for the AFP assay. This study opens up a new way to modulate the fluorescence of native CDs for reversible nano-switch, which may broaden the applications of CDs in versatile bioassays.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Fluorescence
Carbon dots
Redox reaction
Alkaline phosphatase
α-fetoprotein

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pglast pii piinorm pubdateend pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure e-component body acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantnumber grantsponsor grantsponsorid highlightsabst primabst ref specialabst vitae

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Procedures for the enzymatic assay of ALP
The fluorescent nanoswitch based on CDs modulated by the redox reaction and its mechanism
Fluorescence nanoswitch for probing the activity of ALP

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic representation of (A) preparation procedure of Ab2/Au NPs/DNA bioconjugates. (B) Immunoassay preparation on 96-well plates and sandwich-type detection procedure.

(A) TEM image (with magnified image in the insert), (B) XRD pattern, (C) FT–IR spectra, (D) UV–vis absorption spectrum (blue line, left axis) and the fluorescence spectrum (black line, right axis), and (E) the emission spectra of CDs with various excitations. The inset in image (D) shows the photographs of CDs’ solution under a UV lamp. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

The fluorescence of CDs (black line) is quenched by KMnO4 (10μM) (red line) and then restored by AA (0.5mM) (blue line).Inset: Images of CDs (a) in the presence of KMnO4 (b) and the subsequent addition of AA to the quenched fluorescence by KMnO4 (c) under UV light irradiation. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

(A) XPS spectra of CDs, CDs+KMnO4 and CDs+KMnO4 +AA. (B) UV–vis absorption spectra of CDs (black line), KMnO4 (red line), CDs+KMnO4 (pink line) and CDs+KMnO4 +AA (blue line). (C) Fluorescence lifetime of CDs (black line), CDs+KMnO4 (blue line), CDs +KMnO4 +AA (red line). (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
Principle of the fluorescent assay for ALP based on CDs.

The fluorescent immunoassay of AFP. (A) Fluorescence recovery spectra of CDs quenched by KMnO4 in the presence of different concentrations of AFP, from a to g, the concentrations of AFP were 0, 0.01, 0.1, 1, 10, 100, 1000pg/mL. (B) Relationship between F/F0 and the logarithmical concentration of AFP. (C) Selectivity of the fluorescence immunoassay to AFP (0.01ng/mL) compared with other interferents at 0.1ng/mL level: CEA, PSA, and human IgG. The error bars indicate the relative standard deviation of four repeated experiments.

head.ce:title: Tuning surface states to achieve the modulated fluorescence of carbon dots for probing the activity of alkaline phosphatase and immunoassay of α-fetoprotein

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Synthetic and Biological Colloids, Ministry of Education, School of Chemical and Material Engineering, Jiangnan University, Wuxi 214122, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Synthetic and Biological Colloids
School of Chemical and Material Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Public Health Research Center at Jiangnan University, Wuxi 214122, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Public Health Research Center at Jiangnan University

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: Key Laboratory of Synthetic and Biological Colloids, Ministry of Education, School of Chemical and Material Engineering, Jiangnan University, Wuxi 214122, China.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A novel concept to reversibly modulate the fluorescence of carbon dots through redox chemistry is achieved. This reversibly fluorescent nanoswitch is demonstrated to have versatile biosensing applications for probing the enzymatic reaction of ALP and immunoassay of AFP (taken as a model analyte) with high sensitivity. This research may broaden the applications of CDs in versatile bioassays.

A convenient redox reaction was utilized to tune the surface states of carbon dots (CDs), leading to a reversibly fluorescent nanoswitch. The oxidizing treatment of CDs by KMnO4 brought about partial oxidation of hydroxyl groups to carboxyl groups, resulting in effective fluorescence quenching; reversely, the subsequent addition of ascorbic acid (AA) restored the quenched fluorescence by the reduction of the previously oxidized carboxyl groups to hydroxyl groups. Based on the redox modulated fluorescence of CDs, quantitative evaluation of alkaline phosphatase (ALP) activity in a wide range from 0.002 to 250U/L with a low detection limit of 6.0×10−4 U/L was realized. In addition, this testing protocol was also expanded to an immunoassay (taking α-fetoprotein (AFP) as an example) by the cascade amplification strategy combing the hybridization chain reaction (HCR) and the ALP–based biocatalysis. A wide linear range of 1.0 fg/mL to 1.0ng/mL with a quite low detection limit of 0.2 fg/mL were obtained for the AFP assay. This study opens up a new way to modulate the fluorescence of native CDs for reversible nano-switch, which may broaden the applications of CDs in versatile bioassays.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon nanomaterials, a rising star in the research of nanomaterials, have fascinatingoptical merits such as resistance to photobleaching, low-toxicity, good biocompatibility and stability [1]. The unique photophysical properties rendered by carbon dots (CDs) encourage their utilization in fascinating biological applications such as bioimaging and biosensing. In the field of biosensing based on CDs, the classical sensing mechanisms including photoinduced electron transfer (PET) [2], intramolecular charge transfer (ICT) [3], surface energy transfer (SET) [4], and fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) [5] are employed widely. Alternatively, a new kind of detection mode based on reversible fluorescent nanoswitch is designed for biomolecular probing [6]. In the reversible fluorescent nanoswitch sensing system, the fluorescence of CDs is quenched and recovered by controlling the aggregation/redispersion state of CDs. However, generally, all the above detection systems can only work when the CDs and the targets are at a particular distance or under a specific/complementary geometry, rendering the method to be complicated, time-consuming and therefore being limited for practical applications. Thus, to develop CDs-based fluorescent assays with exquisitely modulated strategy is still very demanding.

In contrast to other fluorescent nanomaterials such as semiconductor quantum dots and noble metal nanoclusters, which necessitates surface modification to endow them with water dispersibility and recognition ability, there are abundant functional groups (-OH, CO, COC) on the surface of native CDs. These functional groups render them to be readily dispersible in water and to interact with a wide range of organic and inorganic materials [7–9], greatly widening the scope of CDs’ applications. Interestingly, these functional groups also endow CDs with chemical reducing abilities for some metal ions, which can be used to synthesize composite materials of CDs and metals such as CDs/silver [10], CDs/gold [11,12] and CDs/palladium [13]. Moreover, Zheng et al. [14] found that the quantum yields of CDs was enhanced by reducing the weakly luminescent CDs with the strong reductant of NaBH4. Though the above researches demonstrated the abilities of CDs to interact with some oxidative/reductive agents, the redox property of CDs has never been explored in analytical applications. Herein, we introduce a novel strategy for reversible modulating the fluorescence of CDs. Treatment of CDs by KMnO4 led to fluorescence quenching (switch to “off” state) due to the oxidation of hydroxyl moieties on their surface, while subsequent treatment with ascorbic acid (AA) resulted in the fluorescence recovery (switch to “on” state) due to the restore of the hydroxyl groups. Therefore, a reversible fluorescence nanoswitch can be achieved through the KMnO4/AA controlled redox of the surface state of CDs. This nanoswitch based on the native CDs modulated by the interfacial oxidation−reduction reaction possesses some notable advantages: firstly, native CDs as the key signaling transducer was used without complex modification or doping; secondly, it opens up a facile and novel method for in situ reversibly tuning the fluorescence properties of CDs; moreover, the native CDs with redox tuned fluorescence behaves as a “turn-on” fluorescent sensor for versatile targets including probing the activity of alkaline phosphatase (ALP) and immunoassay of α-fetoprotein (AFP) with high sensitivity. This study not only provides a new platform for the application of CDs in biosensing, but also it provides references for studying the photophysical/photochemical properties of CDs related to their surface states.

Chloroauric acid (HAuCl4·3H2O), bovine serum albumin (BSA), trisodium citrate, Tween–20, ascorbic acid (AA), KMnO4, trypsin, lysozyme, tris-hydroxymethylaminomethane (Tris) hydrochloride, MgCl2·6H2O, ZnCl2, KCl, CaCl2, Na2CO3 were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Alkaline phosphate (ALP), ascorbic acid 2–phosphate (AAP), L-Cystein (Cys), glutathione (GSH) and thrombin were purchased from Shanghai Yuanye Biological Technology Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Strepavidin–alkaline phosphatase conjugate (SA–ALP) was purchased from Beyotime Biotechnology (Haimen, China). Human IgG was obtained from Boster Biological Technology, Co., Ltd. (Wuhan, China). Human α-fetoprotein (AFP), antihuman α-fetoprotein monoclonal antibody, carcinoembryonic antigen (CEA), prostate-specific antigen (PSA) were obtained from Jorferin Biotechnology Co., Ltd. (Beijing, China). The dilution and usage of ALP should be with 0.01mol/L Tris-HCl (pH 8.0) buffer containing 5mM MgCl2 and 0.2mM ZnCl2. A 0.01M phosphate buffer solution (PBS) (pH 7.4) was used for the preparation of antigen (Ag) and antibody (Ab) stock solutions. The washing buffer solution was 0.02M Tris-HCl (pH 7.4) containing 0.05% Tween-20. The blocking buffer was 0.01mol/L Tris-HCl containing 3% (w/v) BSA.
All oligonucleotides were synthesized by Sangon Biotech. Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China) with the sequences of the oligonucleotides as follows:
Capture probe: 5′-SH-(CH2)6-AAAAAAGAAGGAGGGGCGACT-3′
Biotin-H1: 5′-biotin-(CH2)6-GGGGCGACTTGAAACAGTCGCCCCTCCTTC-3′
Biotin-H2: 5′-biotin-(CH2)6-GTTTCAAGTCGCCCCGAAGGAGGGGCGACT-3′
Each oligonucleotide was heated to 90°C for 10min, and slowly cooled down to room temperature before usage.

Fluorescence spectra of CDs in solutions were measured on Cary Eclipse fluorescence spectrophotometer (Varian Co. Ltd., USA). Fluorescence measurements for detecting the activity of ALP and immunoassay of AFP were performed in 96–well plates on SpectraMax M5 multi–mode microplate readers (Molecular Devices, USA). UV–vis absorption spectra were recorded by a TU–1901 spectrophotometer (Beijing Purkinje General Instrument Co. Ltd., China). The attenuated total reflection Fourier transform infrared (ATR-FTIR) spectra were obtained on a Nicolet 6700 spectrometer (Thermo Scientific, USA). X–ray diffraction (XRD) pattern was obtained on an X–ray powder diffractometer with Cu Kα radiation (λ=0.154178nm) at a scanning speed of 4°/min (Bruker AXS, Germany). Fluorescence Lifetime measurements were performed with a photoncounting spectrometer (Edinburgh Instruments FLS920, UK).

Water–soluble CDs were prepared by a one–pot hydrothermal method according to previous report [15]. In the hydrothermal process, ascorbic acid (AA) (1.1g) was dissolved in ultrapure water (25mL) in a 100mL glass beaker, and then equal volumes of ethanol was added into the solution and agitated magnetically to obtain a well–proportioned solution. Subsequently, a certain volume of the mixture was put into the stainless steel autoclave and heated at 180°C for 4h. When cooling to room temperature naturally, a dark brown solution was obtained, indicating the formation of the CDs. After extraction with dichloromethane, the aqueous–phase solution was dialyzed for 24h to remove all impurity molecules. Finally, a yellow aqueous solution containing CDs was obtained. The as prepared CDs were stored at 4°C for future use.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Procedures for the enzymatic assay of ALP
ce:section.ce:para: Firstly, 50μL of AAP (0.1M), 20μL of different concentrations of ALP and 400μL of Tris-HCl solution (pH=8.5) were mixed and incubated for about 25min at 37°C. Subsequently, the mixture of KMnO4 (100μL, 0.1mM) and CDs (100μL) in Tris-HCl solution was added to the above solution and then diluted to 1mL with ultrapure water. The fluorescence intensity of the system was measured with the excitation wavelength of 360nm.

The Au NPs/Ab2/DNA bioconjugates were prepared first for signaling the immunoassay. The Au NPs with 13nm diameter were prepared according to a previous protocol [16]. Briefly, 100mL of 0.01% HAuCl4 solution was boiled with vigorous stirring, followed by the addition of 2.5mL of 1% trisodium citrate solution quickly. The color of the solution turned to be deep red, indicating the formation of Au NPs. After continued stirring and cooling down, the obtained Au NPs were stored in dark at 4°C for future use.

The preparation process of the capture DNA and the second antibody (Ab2) conjugated Au NPs (the Au NPs/Ab2/DNA bioconjugates) is as follows: after adjusting the pH of Au NPs’ solution (1mL) to 7.6, 20μL of 0.1mg/mL anti-AFP antibody (the second antibody, Ab2) was added to the solution, followed by an incubation at 4°C for 2h with slow stirring. The resulting Ab2–attached Au NPs were reacted with 20μL of 100μM capture DNA (activated by adding 10μL of 0.01M TCEP for 2h) for 24h. Afterward, 200μL of 1% BSA blocking solution was added and incubated for 30min. The bioconjugates were centrifuged and washed for three times with ultrapure water. Finally, the Au NPs/Ab2/DNA bioconjugates were dispersed in Tris-HCl buffer solution for use.

The immunoassay for AFP is as follows: A 20μL of 0.1mg/mL anti-AFP antibody (Ab1) was added to the 96 well plates and incubated at 4°C overnight, followed by washing for three times with washing buffer and blocked by BSA blocking solutions for 30min. Next, 20μL of the AFP solution (Ag) with certain concentration was added to the wells and incubated for 1h at 37°C, followed by washing with washing buffer. After the binding reaction between Ab1 and Ag, the Au NPs/Ab2/DNA bioconjugates were injected to the wells and incubated for 1h. After removing the uncombined bioconjugates of Au NPs/Ab2/DNA and washing with washing buffer, 40μL of hybridization buffer (1.0M NaCl, 50mM Tris-HCl, pH 7.4) containing biotinylated hairpin probe H1 (3.0μM) and H2 (3.0μM) were added, and incubated at room temperature for 1h. After rinsed with washing buffer, 20μL of 0.1mg/mL SA–ALPs were added and incubated for 1h and rinsed with washing buffer for three times. Finally, 50μL of AAP (0.1M) and 50μL of Tris-HCl buffer solution (pH=8.5) were added and incubated for 25min before the addition of the mixture of KMnO4 and CDs (100μL/well), and then the fluorescence was measured.

The morphology, composition, and photophysical properties of CDs are characterized. The transmission electron microscopy (TEM) image (shown in Fig. 1 A) indicates that the particle diameters of the as-prepared CDs range from 2 to 3nm. The crystallinity state of CDs is judged from X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern (Fig. 1B), which displays a clear broad peak at around 23°, demonstrating an amorphous property [17] of the CDs prepared in our case. The fourier transform infrared (FT–IR) spectroscopy (Fig. 1C) manifests the surface functional groups present on the as-prepared CDs. The stretching vibrations of OH and CO give rise to characteristic absorption peaks at 3278 and 1638cm−1. The peaks at 1445 and 1101cm−1 are ascribed to the asymmetric and symmetric stretching vibrations of COC [18], respectively. As shown in Fig. 1D, CDs exhibit two typical UV–vis absorption peaks at 260 and 340nm. The former absorption peak, corresponding to the π–π* transition of the aromatic sp2 domains, ought to root in the formation of multiple aromatic chromophores. The latter, attributing to the n–π* transition of the CO bond. The as-synthesized CDs exhibit fluorescence emission peak at 430nm at the excitation wavelength of 360nm, and the CDs aqueous solution emits strongly blue photoluminescence under the UV lamp (Fig. 1D, inset). Though the exact luminescence mechanism of CDs still remains a lot of controversy until now, most researchers attribute the luminescence mechanism to the quantum confinement of the emissive energy traps on the surface of the nanoparticles [19,20]. Similarly to most CDs described in previous literatures [19,20], the emission wavelength of the as–prepared CDs is excitation dependent. With the excitation wavelength increases from 330 to 400nm, the fluorescence spectra of CDs shift to longer wavelengths (Fig. 1E). We reasoned such excitation–dependent fluorescence behavior to the effects of particles of different sizes and considerable distribution of emissive trap sites on each carbon nanoparticles [19].

ce:section.ce:section-title: The fluorescent nanoswitch based on CDs modulated by the redox reaction and its mechanism
ce:section.ce:para: We investigated the initial fluorescence spectra of CDs in the absence and presence of KMnO4. As shown in Fig. 2 , the fluorescence of CDs was quenched effectively after the addition of KMnO4, and the increased concentrations of KMnO4 resulted in a progressive decrease of the fluorescence intensity of CDs (Fig.S1A). The fluorescence quenching efficiency (F0/F) of CDs was directly proportional to the concentration of KMnO4. A good linear relationship was obtained between the fluorescence quenching efficiency and the concentration of KMnO4 in the range from 0.02 to 10μM (Fig.S1B) with the linear regression equation of F0/F=1.0+0.93[KMnO4] (F0 and F are the fluorescence intensity of the CDs in the absence and presence of the KMnO4, respectively). Evidently, this quenching effect of CDs by KMnO4 was accord with the Stern–Volmer equation. Interestingly, the KMnO4 quenched fluorescence of CDs was restored after the introduction of AA (Fig. 2). To obtain a relatively low fluorescence background and good sensitivity for AA, an appropriate concentration of 10μM of KMnO4 was employed to completely turn off the fluorescence of CDs (Fig. 2). The quenched fluorescence of CDs induced by KMnO4 showed sensitive response to AA (Fig.S2). The fluorescence recovery efficiency F/F0 (F0 and F was the fluorescence intensity of CDs quenched by KMnO4 in the absence and presence of AA, respectively) was logarithmically proportional to the concentration of AA in the range of 2.0–900nM with a detection limit of 0.5nM (S/N=3).This ultra-sensitivity of the quenched fluorescence of CDs by KMnO4 to AA enabled us to further establish versatile bioassays.

Up to now, the exact luminescence mechanism of CDs still remains a lot of controversy, for which a widely accepted mechanism for their luminescence emission is the radiative recombination of excitons [21]. Similar to the semiconducting quantum dots, the fluorescence properties of CDs can be affected by various factors, such as element doping [22], the aggregation/disaggregation state [23,24] in solution and their surface states [14]. Especially, the fluorescence of CDs is sensitive to their surface states. As is known, CDs obtained from the hydrothermal method contain different kinds of surface oxygen groups such as hydroxy, carbonyl, carboxy, and epoxy that arise from the different surface oxidation degree of carbon atoms [25,26]. The types of the oxygen-functional groups, forming different “surface states”, have great impacts on the photophysical and photochemical behaviors of CDs [27].

In order to investigate the changes of surface groups on CDs, attenuated total reflection Fourier transform infrared (ATR-FTIR) spectra, XPS analysis and UV–vis absorption spectra were employed to manifest the reactions between KMnO4 or AA and CDs. As can be seen from Fig. 1C, the vibrational absorption band intensity of −OH at 3278cm−1 decreased accompanied by the absorption band intensity of CO at 1638cm−1 increased after oxidation by KMnO4, indicating there were fewer −OH but more CO groups on the surface of the KMnO4 treated CDs than on the native CDs. After the KMnO4 treated CDs were reacted with AA, the vibrational absorption band of −OH and CO were recovered due to the reduction of carbonyl groups back to hydroxyl groups. As shown in Fig. 3 A, XPS analysis shows that the CO peak at 288.2eV increases in intensity upon the addition of KMnO4 whereas the intensity of COH peak at 286.5eV decreases obviously, which indicate that the surface hydroxyl groups are oxidized to carbonyl groups after the reaction between CDs and KMnO4. Furthermore, the destructed surface states of CDs induced by KMnO4 can be restored after the subsequent addition of AA through the reversible redox reaction. As indicated in the XPS spectra (Fig. 3A), the increased intensity of the CO peak at 288.2eV and the decreased intensity of COH peak at 286.5eV are almost back again with the further addition of AA, indicating the reduction of the oxidized CDs. As indicated in Fig. 3B, the original CDs show two typical UV–vis absorption peaks at 260 and 340nm. The peak at 260nm was corresponding to the ππ* transition of the aromatic sp2 domains, the peak at 340nm was attributing to the nπ* transition of the CO bond[14]. After reaction with KMnO4, the typical UV–vis absorption peak at 260nm of CDs disappeared, while the absorption intensity (for CO bond) at 340nm increased obviously, which might be attributed to the oxidation of hydroxyl groups to carbonyl groups on the surface of CDs. After the further reaction with AA, the absorption spectrum of the KMnO4 treated CDs restored again, indicating the recovery of the surface groups of CDs. The above results implied the reversible redox reaction occurred at the surface groups of CDs in the presence of KMnO4 and AA.

In order to find if the reduction product of KMnO4 lead to the fluorescence quenching of CDs, we use XPS to determine the reduction product of KMnO4 after its reaction with CDs, however, there was no signal for the element of Mn. Thus, we ruled out the possibility of the formation of solid MnO2. In addition, no restored fluorescence signal was observed in the presence of H2O2 (1.0×10−5 M) in our detection system, implying that the quenched fluorescence of CDs was not due to the formation of MnO2. If MnO2 was formed after the reaction of CDs with KMnO4, a quenched fluorescence of CDs can be observed and the quenched fluorescence of CDs by MnO2 can be turned on after the addition of H2O2 due to the fast reaction between MnO2 and H2O2 [28]. The manganite ion (MnO4 2−) or Mn2+, possible reduction products for KMnO4 after its reaction with CDs, hardly influenced the fluorescence of CDs in our experiment (data not shown). Thus, we ruled out the fact that it was due to the reduction product of KMnO4 that led to fluorescence quenching of CDs.

The surface states will affect the radiative recombination of excitons and thus influence the luminescence characteristics of CDs. As is known, there are many kinds of surface oxygen groups on the surface of CDs, and they have different effects on the radiative recombination of the excitons. The CO and COOH groups can facilitate the separation of excitons, reducing the rate of recombination. Conversely, COH groups will facilitate the recombination of excitons, increasing the fluorescence emission. Some research has also indicated that the CDs with more COH groups on their surface will exhibit better fluorescence [14,27]. From the above analysis, we attributed the fluorescence quenching and recovery of CDs by KMnO4/AA to the oxidation/reduction of the surface oxygen groups, leading to the enhancement/attenuation of the radiative recombination of the excitons.

In theory, quenching mechanisms are usually classified as either static or dynamic quenching. The former is ascribed to the formation of a nonfluorescent ground-state complex, which will frequently disturb the absorption spectra of the fluorophore, whereas the latter occurs as a result of diffusive encounters between the fluorophore and quencher during the lifetime of the excited state, and thus no changes in the absorption spectrum will be observed [29]. Accordingly, the obvious absorption spectral change inevitably indicated the probable quenching mechanism in our detection was static quenching. In order to further confirm the quenching type of the present system, we also studied fluorescence lifetimes of CDs in the absence/presence of KMnO4 and the subsequent addition of AA. As shown in Fig. 3C, the fluorescence lifetimes of CDs in the absence/presence of KMnO4 and the further introduction of AA were almost identical and the decay curves fitted very well to a single-exponential function with acceptable residuals, and the average lifetime was 2.76ns for free CDs, 2.68ns in the presence of KMnO4 and 2.70ns in the presence of KMnO4 and AA (Table S1). The almost unchanged fluorescence lifetimes proved that static quenching/recovery mechanism controlled over dynamic process, which was accorded with the fact that the surface passivation change leading to the fluorescence quenching/recovery. All the evidences discussed above confirmed that the oxidation/reduction reaction modulated by KMnO4/AA led to the surface structure alteration/restoration of the CDs, which triggered establishment of the native CDs–based fluorescent nanoswitch.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence nanoswitch for probing the activity of ALP
ce:section.ce:para: As an essential enzyme in phosphate metabolism, ALP plays key roles in various biological processes. For example, it catalyzes the hydrolysis of orthophosphoric monoesters into phosphate and the corresponding alcohols/phenols. As one of the typical substrates of ALP, ascorbic acid 2-phosphate (AAP) can be hydrolyzed to AA and phosphate. Since AAP and ALP hardly affected the fluorescence of CDs (Fig.S3), the observed KMnO4–induced fluorescence quenching and the subsequent enzymatically generated product (AA) triggered fluorescence recovery, providing a solid base to fabricate a switchable fluorescence sensor for ALP. The principle of the strategy is illustrated in Fig. 4 .

Several possible factors influencing the assay for probing the activity of ALP were considered, including the type and pH of the buffer solution, incubation temperature, and reaction time. As known, the type and pH values of the buffer solution are important factors in enzymatic processes. In the current study, considering inorganic phosphate may inhibit the activity of ALP [30], Tris-HCl buffer was chosen as the reaction buffer instead of phosphate buffer solution (PBS). Since ALP could only maintain high activity in alkaline conditions, the effect of pH on the system was investigated in the range of 7.5–10.0, and the best result was obtained at pH 8.5 (Fig. S4A). Temperature responses of the enzymatic system from 25 to 40°C was examined (Fig.S4B), and the result confirmed that the most suitable incubation temperature for the hydrolysis of AAP by ALP was 37°C. In addition, the recovered fluorescence intensity gradually increased with the time going on, and the value reached equilibrium after 20min (Fig.S4C).

To investigate the sensitivity of the developed fluorescence assay for quantitative detection of ALP, the fluorescence intensity of CDs quenched by KMnO4 was detected after their incubation with various concentrations of ALP under the optimal detection conditions. As displayed in Fig.S5A, the quenched fluorescence restored progressively with the increase of the activity of ALP. The fluorescence recovery effect (defined as F/F0, where F0 was the fluorescence intensity of CDs quenched by KMnO4 and F was the recovered fluorescence intensity with the catalyzed reaction of ALP) increased linearly upon the logarithm concentrations of ALP over the range of 0.002U/L to 250U/L (Fig.S5B). The limit of detection (LOD) at a signal-to-noise ratio of 3 was 0.6 mU/L. We made a comparison between our assay and other reported methods for the detection of ALP (Table S2). Obviously, our detection limit was not only much lower than other ALP detection methods including colorimetry [31,32], electrochemistry [33], electrochemiluminescence [34], but also much lower than other fluorimetry [23,24,35,36]. Evidently, the proposed method has great advantages and great application prospect in terms of the low cost and low toxicity of the probe, as well as its high sensitivity.

To further test the selectivity of the developed method for ALP detection, we investigated some potential interference including metal ions (Na+, K+, Mg2+, Ca2+), proteins (glucose oxidase (GOx), trypsin, lysozyme, thrombin), amino acids such as glutathione (GSH) and cysteine (Cys), and other molecules (uric acid and vitamin E). As shown in Fig.S5C, these species do not exhibit obvious response in ALP detection except for GSH and Cys, because GSH and Cys are also reducing agents like AA. However, the interference of GSH and Cys could be eliminated by adding N–ethylmaleimide (NEM), a scavenger that can specifically react with sulfhydryl compounds [28]. Thus, the proposed approach showed acceptable selectivity toward ALP detection.

Considering that ALP is a widely used catalytic label in immunoassays, in this work, we also expanded the ALP–catalyzed hydrolysis of AAP based fluorescent nanoswitch to immunoassays by taking AFP as an example. In this assay, hybridization chain reaction–based signal amplification strategy was employed to achieve the ultrasensitive assay of AFP. The principle of the amplification approach and the fluorescent immunoassay is shown in Scheme 1 . Firstly, the anti-AFP antibody (Ab1) was fixed on the 96 well plates and incubated with the antigen (Ag, AFP), then the second antibody (Ab2) and the capture DNA co-conjugated Au NPs (Au NPs/Ab2/DNA) was casted to form a sandwich structure. Subsequently, two complementary hairpins DNAs labeled with biotin named biotin-H1 and biotin-H2 were introduced, the hairpins were stable and would not open or hybridize with each other at room temperature [37]. The capture DNA coated on the surface of Au NPs had specific sequence to open the stem structure of the biotin-H1. The newly exposed sticky end of the biotin-H1 opened the hairpin of biotin-H2 to expose a newly sticky end on biotin-H2, which in turn opened the stem structure on another biotin-H1. In this way, hybridization chain reaction was triggered, where the hybridization events of alternating biotin-H1 and biotin-H2 formed nicked double-helix. Finally, strepavidin–alkaline phosphatase conjugate (SA–ALPs) bond to the strands of DNA through the high affinity reaction between streptavidin and biotin. The labeled SA–ALPs catalyzed the hydrolysis of AAP to AA, inducing the fluorescence recovery of the quenched CDs by KMnO4. The quenched fluorescence intensity increased progressively with the increased concentrations of AFP (Fig. 5 A), and the fluorescence recovery (F/F0) increased linearly with the logarithmical concentration of AFP over the range of 1.0 fg/mL to 1.0ng/mL (Fig. 5B). Based on three times signal–to–noise ratio (S/N=3), a quite low detection limit of 0.2 fg/mL was obtained. As listed in Table S3, this detection limit is much lower than many other assays for AFP detection such as colorimetry [38], electrochemiluminescence [16,39] electrochemistry [40], photoelectrochemistry [41,42] and some fluorimetry [43–45]. Evidently, this strategy for ALP–based immunoassay has some notable features: firstly, the native and benign CDs without complex modification or doping makes the detection more facile and biocompatible, which can ensure ideal signal output. Secondly, the successful establishment of “turn-on” fluorescent nanoswitch further improves the reliability of the detection. Thirdly, combing the sensitive response of CDs to its surface state, the HCR–assisted signal amplification and the subsequent ALP–based biocatalysis for cascade amplification, the proposed bioassay exhibits ultrahigh sensitivity toward AFP. In terms of all the advantages described above, our strategy is very attractive and has promising applications.

As indicated in Fig. 5C, responses from the 10-fold higher concentration of the interfering species of CEA, PSA or human IgG was much lower than that of target AFP, indicating the good specificity and satisfactory selectivity of the developed immunoassay.

The feasibility of this signal amplification strategy for potential clinical application was investigated using several real samples of human sera. In order to cover the wide linear detection range of the proposed assay, these serum samples were appropriately diluted with 10mM phosphate buffer solution, pH 7.4 prior to assay. As listed in Table S4, the levels of AFP analyzed by our method were in good accordance with the reference values obtained by the ELISA method, indicating acceptable feasibility to detect AFP in clinical samples.

In this study, a novel and reversible fluorescent nanoswitch was developed based on modulating the redox state of the surface oxygen functional groups of native CDs using KMnO4/AA. Based on the redox regulated fluorescence of CDs, a “signal–on” fluorescent assay was developed for the highly sensitive and selective detection of ALP and the expanded application of the motif in immunoassays by taking AFP as an example. To the best of our knowledge, this is the first report of native CDs–based redox fluorescent nanoswitch assay that can function for versatile biomolecules. The proposed native CDs–based nanoswitch may further trigger the broad applications of CDs in biological analysis and clinical diagnosis.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (21575052, 21275065, 21676123), the MOE & SAFEA for the 111 Project (B13025), the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities (JUSRP51314B), and the Opening Foundation of the Public Health Centre at Jiangnan University (JUPH201507).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.snb.2017.11.011.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following are Supplementary data to this article:

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Xin Fang is a MS candidate in the School of Chemical and Material Engineering of Jiangnan University, China. Her main research interests are synthesis of nanomaterials and their applications in fluorescent analysis.
Xiao-Qin Li is a MS candidate in the School of Chemical and Material Engineering of Jiangnan University, China. Her main research interests are nanomaterials based biological analysis.
Hong Wang is a MS candidate in the School of Chemical and Material Engineering of Jiangnan University, China. Her main research interests are nanomaterials based biological analysis.

Xiuming Wu is a teacher of School of Chemical and Material Engineering of Jiangnan University, China. She received MSc degree in physical chemistry from Nanjing Normal University in 2009. Her main research areas are the synthesis of nanomaterials and development of biosensors.

Guang-Li Wang is a professor of School of Chemical and Material Engineering of Jiangnan University, China. She received a PhD degree in analytical chemistry from Nanjing University in 2009. The main research interests of Dr. Wang are synthesis of nanomaterials and their applications in photoelectrochemical, fluorescent bioassays and enzyme mimetics.
